title: Hand-lettered journal headers
subtitle: Writing titles and headings in your journal with consistent weight, spacing, and style
excerpt: A hand-lettered header gives a journal page a clear visual hierarchy and makes the spread easier to navigate. This tutorial covers three approaches: a simple single-weight brushed header, a faux-calligraphy thickened header, and a block letter outline header. Each works with tools already in a standard journalling kit.
sourceNotes: Hand lettering for journals is a widely practised contemporary technique. The three approaches described here are a synthesis of brush lettering, faux calligraphy, and block letter methods documented in modern lettering manuals and widely available in pre-copyright instructional materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hand-lettering technique that mimics the thick-and-thin character of calligraphy without a calligraphy nib. Letters are written with any pen, then the downstrokes are identified and thickened by adding a second line parallel to the original and filling in between.
The height of capital letters measured from the baseline. Ruling consistent cap-height lines ensures all letters in a heading sit at the same level.

Writing with a brush pen, in which pressure on the downstroke produces thick lines and light pressure on the upstroke produces thin lines. The same pressure-and-release principle as calligraphy, but with a flexible brush tip rather than a split nib.

content.text: A journal header does one thing: it tells the reader at a glance what this page or spread is about. The lettering style matters less than the consistency of scale, the spacing between letters, and the alignment to a ruled baseline. This tutorial teaches three practical approaches, from fastest to most polished:
with any fine liner, and a block outline letter. All three can be done within the journal page itself without any special equipment.

items.name: Brush pen with a flexible tip (for brush lettering headers)
Fine liner, 0.3 mm or 0.5 mm (for faux calligraphy and block outlines)
Pencil and eraser (for light guide sketching before inking)
Metal ruler (for ruling baseline and cap-height lines)

content.text: Rule a baseline in pencil at the intended header height. Rule a
line above it: 8 to 12 mm above the baseline gives a comfortable heading size.

Write the heading with the brush pen, applying pressure on the downstrokes and using light strokes for upstrokes and curves. Keep the letter heights consistent to the ruled cap-height line. Allow 2 to 3 mm spacing between letters.
Once dry, erase the pencil guide lines. Add a thin shadow line below each letter with a fine liner for depth if wanted.
Write the heading in pencil at the intended size. Identify every downstroke in each letter: the parts of the letter that go from top to bottom.
Draw a second line parallel to each downstroke, 1 to 2 mm to the right of the original. Fill in the space between the original stroke and the new line to create a thick downstroke.
Ink the final outlines with a fine liner, including the thickened downstrokes. Erase the pencil guide.

Draw each letter in pencil as a simple outlined rectangle or box shape. The strokes are not single lines but outlined shapes. Ink the outline with a fine liner, leaving the interior white or colouring it with markers. Block letters are the most forgiving for inconsistent line work because the structure is defined by the outline, not the fill.
